TPA-induced LNCaP apoPtosis was preceded by rapid yet transient induction of the early response transcription factors NGFI-A and c-fos. In dose-response experiments, NGFI-A and c-fos gene activation parallels the induction of death in LNCaP cells. TPA-induced expression of NGFI-A and c-fos and death of LNCaP cultures were blocked by pretreatment with staurosporine, a potent inhibitor of several protein kinases. Based on these studies, we suggest that activation of a TPA-inducible kinase(s) mediates apoptosis of androgen-sensitive prostate cells by means of an intracellular pathway that may involve the transient activation of the early response transcription factors NGFI-A and c-fos.

texts.full_text: 178 patients with penetrating eye injuries treated between July 1996 and June 2006 were reviewed. Twenty-four patients (14%) had open globe injuries related to nail-guns. Demographic data, ocular examination findings, and visual outcomes were recorded. Of the 24 cases identified, 17 were work-related; this represented approximately 71% of patients. All were men with an average age of 29.4 years. Fifty-eight percent of the patients were non-English speaking. There was no record of any patient wearing safety glasses at the time of injury. The visual acuity at presentation ranged from 20/20 to hand motion or worse. Half (50%) of these patients had a presenting visual acuity of hand motion or worse. At last examination, 38% (nine patients) had visual acuity of 20/40 or better, 34% (eight patients) had visual acuity of counting fingers to hand motion, 13% (three patients) 20/60, 4% (one patient) 20/200, and three were lost to follow-up. The correlation coefficient between visual acuity at presentation and most recent was significant at 0.679 (p = 0.001). The majority of nail-gun injuries presented in this series were work-related. These injuries could be prevented with adherence to established safety measures including proper training and education of nail-gun operators, the use of sequential-trip triggers,

texts.full_text: irrational number is not normal1234567890, then every possible sequence of numerals appears in its infinite decimal expansion. And so if the universe could be considered infinitely "not-normal," it would indeed contain everything imaginable—an endless repetition and variation on all themes conceivable by human (and perhaps non-human) minds. Imagine an infinity where planet-sized enterprises fail to thrive; carpet-cleaning operations, for instance, might falter due to inefficiency or lack of customers. Entire planets could then be devoted solely to those who sold machines used in these failing businesses—machines that were themselves obsolete before they even left the factory floor because no one needed them. These scenarios branch out into ever more convoluted chains: galaxies full of professionals catering indirectly and tangentially, such as psychiatrists for parking meter attendants ticketing veterinarians treating sick dogs belonging to those few remaining customers from failing carpet-cleaning ventures. The absurdity escalates until it reaches a darkly humorous endpoint ("ad suicidem"), where the very concept becomes an exercise in futility within its own infinite expanse, mocking our attempts at order and purpose amidst boundless chaos1234567890 This writeup has been cooled by: robwicksX 1 C! texts.full_text: which has been around since Roman times, and it feels as though you've stepped back into a different era. The Hotel Praktik Rambla offers an unforgettable experience for guests who appreciate the blend of history with modern amenities that make their stay comfortable. The hotel is housed in two historic buildings: one from 1912 (the Palau de la Música) and another built between 1890-91, which was originally a bank. The rooms at Hotel Praktik Rambla are designed to reflect the grandeur of its past while providing guests with all modern conveniences. The interiors feature elegant furniture pieces that have been carefully selected for their beauty as well as functionality—think ornate wooden beds and antique mirrors adorned by delicate floral patterns, paired alongside sleek contemporary fixtures. The hotel's location is also a significant draw: it’s just steps away from the famous Las Ramblas street in Barcelona. This bustling pedestrian avenue offers an array of shops, restaurants with outdoor seating areas where you can enjoy tapas while watching people passby or simply relax and soak up some sun on one its many benches. texts.full_text: ovince of the Colombian Department of Boyacá. The prvince was created in 1991 and its capital city is Tunja. Geography This region has an area of ​​2,200 km² (850 sq mi) with a population density of about 50 inhabitants per square kilometer. The Central Province borders to the north by the province of Neiva; eastward it limits on the departmental territory and southwards in Boyacá. To west is bordered by Santander. History In 1991, after several years of discussion between Tunja (the capital) and Chiquinquiria about which city should be designated as provincial headquarters Central Province was created with its own government. The province has a population density of approximately fifty inhabitants per square kilometer. The main economic activities are agriculture and livestock. Administrative divisions Central Boyacá is divided into 15 municipalities, each municipality consists of one or more corregimientos (corresponding to the municipal sections in other countries) which are listed below with their codes as given by DANE. texts.full_text: y el estonio les aportó la determinación y la fuerza para luchar por la independencia y volveConmemorar los eventos de 1941 en los Estados Bálticos es una tarea que nos obliga, como sociedad civilizada e informada sobre nuestras propias historías trascendentales. La solemnidad del aniversario se ve reafirmado cuando recordamos el sufrimiento y la pérdida de cientos mil vidas inocentes. Las deportaciones masivas que comenzaron en 1941, representan uno de los capaces exponentes de las crueldades humanas bajo un régimen totalitario. La sovieta estaba buscando erradicar a todos aquellos considerados desafíos políticos y sociales dentro sus fronteras ocupadas, incluyendo la “intelligencia” local que podria ofrecer resistencias al control. La pérdida de liderazgo social-politico fue significativa; entre los deportados se encontraban científicos destacados y defensores incansables del pensamiento libre. La acción sovieta no solo buscaba acabar con la resistencia polita, sino tambien destruir las raices culturales que mantuvieron unido a estas naciones bálticas. La memoria vivificante y el recuerdo activo son fundamentos para prevenir futuros crímenes contra humanidad. Es esencial reconocer los sufrimientos pasados, condenar las acciones de aquellos que cometieron tales crueldades sin piedad o compasión humana. La lucha por la independencia y el regreso a una identitat nacional auténtica fue un logro posible gracias al coraje e intransigencia del propio people, quienes enfrentaron las amenazas de su libertad. texts.full_text: Current Bibliography surveys recent publications in the history of science. It covers all disciplines and time periods. The bibliography was begun in 1913 by the historian of science George Sarton as part of his new journal, Isis, and it has been published continuously since then. In 2000 the bibliography office moved to the University of Oklahoma. The print version of the bibliography appears annually and is mailed with the December issue of the journal Isis. Access to the bibliographic data is also available online through OCLC’s HistSciTechMed database, which can be consulted through many libraries in North America and some institutions outside of North America. The Isis Current Bibliography (CB) serves as an essential resource for scholars interested in tracking new publications within their fields. It includes books, articles from scholarly journals, reviews of these works, obituaries relevant to the history of science, bibliographies pertaining specifically or generally to aspects of this field, and catalogues that list collections significant to its study. For more information about how you can access specific entries in our database or contribute your own publications for inclusion within it, please feel free to contact us via email at isisbib@ou.edu. We welcome contributions from researchers around the world who are working on topics related to any aspect of science and technology throughout history. If there's a particular area where you would like more detailed information—such as submission guidelines or details about our digital projects—or if you have questions regarding specific entries in past issues, don't hesitate to reach out. texts.full_text: and you will see that you need to change all the 'x:' prefixes in your code to 'wpf:' to make it compile. The 'c:' prefix references code of your own. Say you have a class library that compiles to MyLib.dll. This library contains a class named MyData. To be able to reference the MyData class you need something like: xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyClasses;assembly=MyLib" in your XAML header. You can then reference the MyData class in you XAML with c:MyData. But you are entirely free to change the 'c' to 'myfabulousclasses' or anything else you fancy. The purpose of this? To distinguish between elements and classes from different namespaces, allowing for a more organized and flexible way to reference them in your XAML code without having to use their fully qualified names. This enables the creation of concise yet readable markup that is easier to maintain. By choosing meaningful prefixes like 'x' or custom ones such as 'c', developers can quickly identify where elements originate from, whether it's part of a standard framework (like WPF) namespace ('http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml') used for built-in controls and markup extensions with the prefix typically set to "x", including key properties like x:Name or converters referenced by an 'x' such as <BooleanToVisibilityConverter>, which utilizes these prefixes. texts.full_text: Q: What is the correct isolation level for Order header - Order lines transactions? Lets say we have a usual situation with Order_Header and Order_LineItems tables. Then the number of dice that John has at the end is a random variable $Y$ defined by $$ Y = X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + \cdots + X_N. $$ A good thing about $X_k$ is it is a Bernoulli variable, so if you can just figure out the probability that John gets the $k$th die, you know all you need to know about $X_k$ (its expectation and variance). ThEre are a number of other difficult-looking problems for which this kind of decomposition is useful. As for the probability that John gets the $k$th die, just consider that the die will be rolled repeatedly until either it rolls a number that John calls out or it rolls a number that Petr calls out. The probability for that particular die to end up in John's possession (or Petr's) on a particular roll is simply a matter of whether it is still on the table and whether it shows the same number John (or Petr) called out. But while the U.S. stock market has been volatile since the 1987 crash, it has never seen another drop of that magnitude. In fact, since the crash, the Dow has gained more than 1,500 percent. And while the S&P 500 has fallen more than 20 percent twice since 1987 — during the 2000-2002 bear market and the 2007-2009 financial crisis — it has never seen a one-day drop of more than 10 percent. Pruitt A Sensory History of the City? The editors' introduction to The City and the Senses is a call for sensory history--a call that has been made before but not answered as frequently or fully as it might have.[1] As they rightly point out, this approach is especially useful in understanding urban culture because "the city's physical presence was experienced primarily through its impact on the senses" (p. 2). The editors' definition of sensory history includes two parts: one focused specifically on sensation and sense perception--what Alexander Cowan calls "sensory experience"--and another that focuses more generally on the cultural meanings associated with the urban environment, including but not limited to those things perceived through the senses.[2] Thus it is less a call for an exclusively sensory history than it is a plea for greater attention within all kinds of historical analysis (including social and political) toward "sensory experience" as one important element. In other words, they are arguing that urban historians should take into account how cities look to the eyes or sound in our ears when we attempt any kind of interpretation. Is he an NCO or officer leading his troops, or just a GI going about his business? I usually like to focus on the common soldier, the everyman in a war zone, because I think that’s the most compelling part of war in general, and with Americans in WW2 in particular: common men in uncommon situations. So, is he shooting? Shooting while standing or shooting while kneeling? What’s he shooting with? M1 Garand? Thompson? BAR? Pistol? Or is he in an MG team? Or maybe he’s not shooting; maybe he’s looking for targets? Or maybe he’s looking for his buddy coming up behind him? texts.full_text: Rockroses, in the genus Cistus, are mainstays in the drought-tolerant gardens of recent years. They hail from the Mediterranean and are well-adapted to the same sort of climate regime here in Southern California, where the cool, usually rainy season of winter gives way to a dry spell of many months through late spring and early fall. Even in coastal areas with more marine moisture, these handsome shrubs perform well, adding evergreen foliage and bright floral displays to the landscape. Less well-known are a couple of their close relatives that are equally adaptable and lovely to look at. Also native to the Mediterranean region — Spain, Portugal, France, and North Africa — they thrive on poor rocky soil. There is one species, Helianthemum nummularium, from which many selections and hybrids have been developed. Most are low-growing shrubs, reaching only six to eight inches in height, but spreading to as As much as two to three feet wide. These plants are commonly known as sunroses or rockroses, similar to their Cistus cousins, but they are distinct in their growth habit and flowers. Sunroses produce small, cup-shaped flowers in a variety of colors, including yellow, orange, pink, and white, which bloom profusely in the late spring and early summer. Another notable relative is Halimium, also known as the yellow rockrose. Halimium species are slightly larger than Helianthemum, growing up to two feet tall and wide. They produce vibrant yellow flowers that resemble small, delicate roses, providing a striking contrast against their dark green, leathery foliage. Halimium is particularly valued for its ability to thrive in harsh, rocky conditions and for its long blooming period. Both Helianthemum and Halimium are excellent choices for rock gardens, slopes, and any sunny, well-drained location. They require minimal water once established and are relatively pest-free, making them ideal for low-maintenance landscapes. Their compact size and vibrant blooms also make them suitable for container gardening, adding a splash of color to patios and balconies. In summary,
